claims.claim_text: The opening vignette presents a specific Argo migration story with exact failure counts, reliability gains, deployment-frequency gains, and dollar-value outcomes.
claims.anchor_text: The data science team lead stared at the Airflow logs in frustration. Their ML training pipeline had failed again—the 47th failure this month—because a Python dependency on one worker didn't match the others. "We spend more time fixing the pipeline than training models," she told the VP of Engineering. After evaluating alternatives, they migrated to Argo Workflows running on Kubernetes. Each step ran in its own container with pinned dependencies. Within three months, pipeline reliability went from 62% to 99.1%. Model deployment frequency increased from weekly to daily. The team calculated the value: faster model iterations meant **$2.8 million in additional revenue** from improved recommendation accuracy, while engineering time saved on pipeline debugging was worth **$340,000 per year**.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that run ML pipelines on inconsistent worker environments often spend significant time debugging dependency drift instead of training and shipping models. Moving each step into a pinned container on Kubernetes can make those workflows more reproducible and improve reliability.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with precise operational and financial figures, so it cannot remain as a factual story without a source.

claims.claim_text: Argo Workflows is a container-native workflow engine for orchestrating parallel jobs on Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The official Argo Workflows overview states this directly.
Tekton’s official overview describes Tekton as a cloud-native solution for building CI/CD pipelines.

claims.claim_text: Argo Workflows supports DAG-based workflows and is used for machine-learning and data-processing workloads.
claims.rationale: The official Argo Workflows overview explicitly mentions DAGs, machine learning, and data processing.

claims.claim_text: The module says Argo Workflows powers ML pipelines at Intuit, Google, NVIDIA, and GitHub.
claims.anchor_text: It powers machine learning pipelines at companies like Intuit, Google, NVIDIA, and GitHub.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For example, Argo Workflows is used in industry for machine-learning and data-processing pipelines.
claims.rationale: Official Argo sources support the general industry/ML use case, but not this specific named-company list.

claims.claim_text: The module says Kubeflow Pipelines uses Argo Workflows as its execution layer.
claims.anchor_text: **Argo Workflows powers Kubeflow Pipelines**—the ML workflow engine uses Argo Workflows as its execution layer
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Argo Workflows is commonly used for Kubernetes-based machine-learning workflows**
claims.rationale: The readily findable Kubeflow primary source is a legacy v1 page, so the broad present-tense statement should be narrowed rather than cited as-is.

claims.claim_text: The module says GitHub Actions is built on Argo Workflows concepts.
claims.anchor_text: **GitHub Actions is built on Argo Workflows concepts**—many of the same DAG patterns apply
claims.suggested_rewrite: **GitHub Actions and Argo Workflows both support dependency-driven workflows, but they are separate systems**
claims.rationale: No primary source supports the provenance claim that GitHub Actions is built on Argo Workflows concepts.

claims.claim_text: The module says Argo Workflows can run 10,000+ pods in a single workflow.
claims.anchor_text: **Argo Workflows can run 10,000+ pods in a single workflow**—it's designed for massive parallel data processing
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Argo Workflows is designed for very large workflows, but you should validate cluster and controller capacity before assuming that scale**
claims.rationale: Official Argo documentation supports large-scale operation with tuning, but not this exact uncited pod-count formulation.

claims.claim_text: The Argo project includes four tools: Workflows, Argo CD, Events, and Rollouts.
claims.rationale: The Argo project homepage lists these four tools.

claims.claim_text: The war-story heading asserts a specific $1.2 million 10,000-pod meltdown incident.
claims.anchor_text: War Story: The $1.2 Million 10,000 Pod Meltdown
claims.suggested_rewrite: War Story: Uncontrolled Parallelism Can Overload a Cluster
claims.rationale: The heading itself makes a precise, unattributed incident-cost claim.

claims.claim_text: The war-story setup describes a specific backtesting incident with exact workload size, time estimate, and cluster size.
claims.anchor_text: A quantitative trading firm needed to backtest 10,000 trading strategy variations across 5 years of market data. Their data science team designed an Argo Workflow that would fan out to 10,000 parallel pods—one per strategy variant—each processing the historical data and outputting performance metrics. On paper, it would complete in 2 hours using their 500-node Kubernetes cluster.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A large fan-out workflow can overwhelm a cluster if you launch too many pods at once without parallelism caps, quotas, and capacity testing.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with fake-precise workload and infrastructure numbers.

claims.claim_text: Large Kubernetes clusters require careful control-plane and etcd sizing because cluster capacity is finite.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation on large clusters explicitly covers supported scale targets and control-plane/etcd sizing considerations.

claims.claim_text: Dagger provides programmable software-delivery pipelines that can run locally, in CI, or in the cloud.
claims.rationale: The official Dagger repository README describes Dagger as programmable software delivery that runs locally, in CI, or in the cloud.
Tekton’s official overview presents Tekton as a cloud-native solution for building CI/CD pipelines on Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: Argo Workflows provides DAG-based workflow orchestration on Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The official Argo Workflows overview states that it supports DAG and step-based workflows on Kubernetes.

further_reading.title: Argo Workflows Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary overview for Argo Workflows concepts, use cases, and positioning.

further_reading.title: Argo Workflows: Running At Massive Scale
further_reading.why_relevant: Relevant for teaching safe parallelism, controller scaling, and cluster-capacity limits.

further_reading.title: Tekton Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful counterpart for the module’s Argo-versus-Tekton comparisons.

notes: Code blocks, tables, quiz answers, and exercise steps were excluded per schema. The broad Kubeflow Pipelines bullet was not kept as a cite-needed claim because the easily verifiable Kubeflow source is a legacy v1 page and does not cleanly support the present-tense wording.
